---
title: "Tropes, Archetypes & Variations"
aliases: ["Character Archetypes", "Trope Subversion", "Archetype Catalogue"]
family: "Story, Character & Voice"
media: [interactive-fiction, live-game, tabletop]
facets:
  effects: [narrative-shorthand, emotional-recognition, earned-surprise]
  problems: [flat-cliche, hollow-subversion, archetype-confusion]
  components: [archetype-role, trope-set, subversion-technique, migration-map]
  genre: [fantasy, mystery, horror, sci-fi]
provenance: "researched"
---

*Silhouette: The shared cast of roles every genre draws from, and the honest ways to bend them.*

Underneath the seven genres sits one layer they all share. Strip the spaceship off the captain and the staff off the wizard and you find the same role doing the same job. It is the figure who knows more than the hero and points the way forward. That is the Mentor, a role older than any genre that borrows it. Tropes and archetypes are the parts an audience already recognises. That recognition is the whole point. A reader who meets the Mentor already knows the shape of the help on offer, before a word is spoken.

The reflex of a certain kind of writer is to treat every familiar pattern as a failure of nerve. That reflex is wrong. A trope is a tool, not a crime. It carries meaning fast, without the writer having to build it from nothing each time. The skill is not in avoiding the pattern but in knowing what it does, then choosing to play it straight or to bend it on purpose.

This article is about that layer. It covers the archetypes the genres all inherit, the case for tropes as tools, and a small set of techniques for bending a pattern without breaking what made it work. It sits under the seven genre articles, because each of them is one local dialect of this shared grammar.

## The Mechanism (How it works)

### Where archetypes come from

The word archetype reaches the craft through two thinkers, and it helps to keep them apart. Carl Jung used it for the recurring figures that surface across the myths and dreams of unrelated cultures. He placed these patterns in what he called the collective unconscious, the inherited layer of mind below personal memory. [researched] His named figures still run through character work. Among them are the Shadow (the disowned dark side of a personality) and the Mentor or wise old guide. The Trickster belongs here too, as does the pairing of anima and animus, the contrasexual image each person holds of the other sex. [researched] Jung's claim was psychological, about minds rather than stories, but the figures transferred cleanly to fiction because fiction is where those minds put their dreams.

Joseph Campbell took the next step. In *The Hero with a Thousand Faces* he argued that heroic myths from every culture share one underlying path, a monomyth of departure, initiation and return. His one-sentence version: a hero ventures from the ordinary world into a region of wonder and wins a decisive victory there. The hero then comes back able to grant gifts to others. [researched] The structural side of that journey is its own subject, the beat-level craft of [Beat Taxonomies & Craft Models](../structure-pacing/beat-taxonomies-and-craft-models.qmd); what concerns us here is the cast Campbell's path implies.

Christopher Vogler turned Campbell's cast into a working toolkit for screenwriters, and his eight figures are the version most designers actually use. The Hero, whose journey is a search for wholeness. The Mentor, who guides. The Threshold Guardian, who tests readiness at the gate. The Herald, who carries the call to adventure. The Shapeshifter, whose loyalty stays uncertain. The Shadow, the antagonist or the hero's own dark potential. The Ally, who helps through the change. The Trickster, who punctures the hero's ego and pushes for change through mischief. [researched] Vogler's insight was that these are *functions*, not fixed personalities. One character can wear two of them, and a character can change function across a story. The figure who arrives as Herald can stay on as Ally.

### Tropes work as tools

A trope is any device an audience recognises, whether a stock situation or a recurring image, perhaps a character role with known behaviour. The mistake is to read recognisable as lazy. The TV Tropes community catalogued tens of thousands of them and settled on the position worth holding, that tropes are tools. A writer uses them to set and steer audience expectation. A device used before is no automatic cliché. [researched] The cliché is a trope used without thought, where the writer reached for the default because it was nearest. The same device used on purpose, because the writer wanted exactly the response it triggers, is craft.

This matters most in interactive work, where the audience meets the cast through choice rather than through a fixed reading. An archetype the player recognises does real work on first contact. Label a character as the Mentor through how they speak and stand, and the player knows to listen and to expect guidance. They grant the figure trust the writer has not yet had to earn. That recognition is a budget the trope hands you for free. The voice work that makes the recognition land, the idiolect and the marker that say "wise guide" before the content does, is [Character Voice](character-voice.qmd); the construction of these figures as people the player can invest in rather than slots is [NPC Craft & Voicing](npc-craft-and-voicing.qmd).

The honest test for whether a trope is earning its place is simple. Ask what the audience feels when they recognise it, and whether you want them to feel that. A trope works because it encodes an emotional contract. The Mentor promises that help is coming. The Betrayer, once recognised, promises that trust will be broken. Spend the recognition deliberately and the trope is a tool. Reach for it by reflex and it is the cliché the nervous writer was right to fear.

::: {.callout-tip}
### The Field Note
The fastest way to tell a working trope from a lazy one is to look at where the writer's attention went. A lazy trope is load-bearing and unexamined. The story rests its whole weight on "he is the Chosen One" and never asks what that does to a child told at twelve that the world is their responsibility. A working trope is either doing quiet structural work in the background or it is the very thing the writer chose to interrogate. If a familiar pattern is carrying weight and you have never once turned it over to look underneath, that is the one to worry about, rather than the trope that simply sits there and does its job. [synthesis]
:::

### The archetype catalogue, and its variations

The named heroic figures are only the spine. Most of a cast is relationship roles, defined by how a character stands to the protagonist, and each of those roles fans out into variations that change its weight. [corpus]

The Betrayer is the clearest case, because the variation decides everything about how the turn lands. The Sleeper was always working for the other side, so every earlier kindness was performance, and the reveal is at its most devastating when the player invested in the kindness. The Ideological Betrayer genuinely believes the betrayal is right, which makes them the most morally complex version, since they are not a liar but a true believer on the wrong side of the protagonist. The Coerced Betrayer wants to stay loyal and cannot, and the Gradual Turn lets the audience watch loyalty erode while the protagonist stays blind. [corpus] In interactive work the choice of variation is partly the player's to make, because the betrayal lands hardest against a relationship the player chose to build, which is the connective tissue between this layer and [Conflict Patterns](conflict-patterns.qmd).

The supporting cast carries its own set of functional roles, most of them straight from the Jungian and Campbell lineage. The Threshold Guardian who tests readiness at the gate, the Trickster who disrupts to reveal, the Herald who announces that change is coming, the Shapeshifter whose allegiance keeps moving. [corpus] Each blocks, disrupts, signals or destabilises, and naming the function tells you what the character is *for* before you decide who they are. A character with no function beyond delivering lore is the exposition fountain, and the cure is to give them an agenda that filters what they reveal, which is a matter of [Exposition & Subtext](exposition-and-subtext.qmd).

### The same role, dressed by genre

The reason this layer sits beneath the genres is that the roles migrate. The Mentor is not a fantasy character who happens to appear elsewhere. It is a function that re-skins to fit whatever world it lands in, while keeping its job. The table below tracks the migration. It also works as a casting prompt: pick the function the story needs, then read across to the costume the genre supplies.

| Archetype | Fantasy | Sci-fi | Mystery | Horror | Romance |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mentor | Wise wizard | Ship's captain, AI guide | Retired detective, informant | Warning voice, last survivor | Experienced friend, therapist |
| Chosen One | Prophesied hero | Engineered saviour | The one witness | The final girl | The one who changes everything |
| Companion | Loyal squire | Crew member | The Watson figure | The sceptic friend | Best friend, confidant |
| Betrayer | Trusted advisor | Corporate mole | The least-suspected suspect | The infected friend | The unfaithful partner |
| Trickster | Bard, fey creature | Hacker, rogue AI | Con artist, fence | Demon, deal-maker | The matchmaker with schemes |
| Threshold Guardian | Dragon, riddle-keeper | Quarantine officer, AI lockout | Hostile witness | The locked door | The disapproving parent |

[corpus]

Reading the table sideways is the genre lens at work. The wise wizard of [Fantasy Conventions](fantasy-conventions.qmd), the AI guide of [Science Fiction Conventions](science-fiction-conventions.qmd), the retired detective of [Mystery & Detective Fiction](mystery-and-detective-fiction.qmd) and the last survivor of [Horror Conventions](horror-conventions.qmd) are one function in four coats. This is also why archetypes are the strongest cross-media lever the family has. The role survives the jump from prose to the table to a live space, because it is defined by what it does, not by the medium it appears in. At a tabletop the Mentor is an improvised non-player character; in a live game they are a costumed actor or a planted clue; in interactive fiction they are a branch of dialogue. The function holds in each. Where the medium changes the role most is in the player's power to move a character between functions: a figure who opens as Mentor can be pushed by player choice toward Betrayer, so the archetype becomes a track the character runs along rather than a fixed label. The bundling of such a role into a selectable identity at the table is [Playbook Design](../systems-mechanics/playbook-design.qmd); systems built explicitly around archetype play, such as the comedy-dystopia roster, sit in the [TTRPG Format Taxonomy](../systems-mechanics/ttrpg-format-taxonomy.qmd).

### Bending a pattern without breaking it

Subversion is where most of the bad work lives, because the word gets read as "do the opposite" when the craft is narrower than that. There is a small, named set of techniques, and each does a different job.

Inversion swaps the archetype's defining quality for its opposite. The Chosen One is now destined to destroy rather than save; the Mentor turns out incompetent and learns alongside the student. [corpus] Deconstruction is the heavier move. It shows the realistic consequences the archetype usually hides. A Chosen One told at twelve to save the world is left with the trauma, resentment and impostor syndrome that the straight version skips; a Mentor who repeatedly recruits children into danger is, from one angle, a predator. [corpus] Perspective shift hands the story to the figure who normally gets none, the villain with reasons or the bystander whose home the hero's battle destroyed. Genre hybridisation drops a role from one genre into the rules of another, so the wise wizard becomes the noir informant who knows too much and will be silenced for it. [corpus]

Then there are the techniques that work on the audience's awareness rather than the trope itself, and these come from the community catalogue. The lampshade is the writer naming a too-convenient or too-familiar moment inside the story and moving on, which buys back the audience's trust by showing the writer saw it too. [researched] A subversion proper is a bait-and-switch. Set the trope up so the audience expects the usual payoff, then deny it. The double subversion goes one step further. It sets up the trope and denies it, then delivers it after all. This lets the writer keep the trope's plot work while still pulling the surprise. [researched] Reconstruction is the move after the cynical years. The writer has watched the trope get subverted to exhaustion and now plays it straight again. This time they are armed with the subversion's lessons. They build the trope back so it has reason to be there rather than assuming it. [researched]

The discipline under all of these is that subversion is only honest when it understands why the trope worked. A trope encodes an emotional contract, and a subversion that ignores the contract is just a broken promise. [synthesis] Invert the Chosen One into a destroyer and the prophecy weight still has to pay off emotionally, or the inversion reads as a stunt. The strongest subversions fulfil the trope's emotional truth by a fresh route rather than refusing to pay it at all. Playing a trope straight, with full awareness of what it does, is frequently the better craft choice than bending it. A clean Mentor beats a clumsy anti-Mentor every time.

::: {.callout-note}
### The Specimen
*Paranoia* runs its whole table on archetype play held one notch from collapse. Every player is a Troubleshooter in a comedy-dystopia where the computer is benevolent and lethal, each is a secret traitor, and the roster of stock roles (the loyal citizen, the mutant, the secret-society member) is the engine rather than the decoration. The game works because it treats the archetypes as a system the players manipulate openly. Everyone at the table knows the Betrayer trope is present, and the comedy comes from watching it play out anyway. It is a worked example of the principle that an archetype the audience recognises does not lose its force from being seen. Naming the trope and running it on purpose is its own kind of craft, distinct from hiding it. [corpus]
:::

## Where this fits

**The bigger picture.** This article is the cross-genre floor the seven genre articles stand on. Each genre is a local dialect of one shared grammar of roles, and the archetype catalogue plus the subversion toolkit is that grammar written out plainly. It sits one level below [A Taxonomy of Genre Conventions](a-taxonomy-of-genre-conventions.qmd), which routes between the genres, and it feeds directly into how a cast is voiced and how its conflicts are built. The reason it carries across media better than almost anything else in the family is that an archetype is a function rather than a surface: the Mentor is the same job whether it speaks in prose, sits at a table or walks a live space in costume. Get the function right and the genre coat is a matter of dressing; get it wrong and no amount of genre flavour will make the character read as a person.

**Next steps.** To see one genre's dialect of this grammar in full, start from [Fantasy Conventions](fantasy-conventions.qmd) (where the Mentor and Chosen One live most natively) or [Mystery & Detective Fiction](mystery-and-detective-fiction.qmd) (where the Betrayer becomes the least-suspected suspect). To turn an archetype into a character who sounds like themselves, the work is [Character Voice](character-voice.qmd) and then [NPC Craft & Voicing](npc-craft-and-voicing.qmd). To make the relationships between these roles generate story rather than sit static, the next article is [Conflict Patterns](conflict-patterns.qmd); to set the roles inside a consistent world that earns them, turn to [Worldbuilding Patterns](worldbuilding-patterns.qmd).

## Sources

- Jung, Carl Gustav. *The Archetypes and the Collective Unconscious* (Collected Works, Vol. 9 Part 1). Princeton University Press, 1959. (Archetypes, the Shadow, anima and animus.) Wikipedia summary: <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Collective_unconscious>
- Campbell, Joseph. *The Hero with a Thousand Faces*. Pantheon Books, 1949. (The monomyth: departure, initiation, return.) <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Hero_with_a_Thousand_Faces>
- Vogler, Christopher. *The Writer's Journey: Mythic Structure for Writers*. Michael Wiese Productions, 1992 (3rd ed. 2007). (The eight character archetypes as functions.) <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Writer%27s_Journey:_Mythic_Structure_for_Writers>
- "Tropes Are Tools." TV Tropes. <https://tvtropes.org/pmwiki/pmwiki.php/Administrivia/TropesAreTools>
- "Subverted Trope." TV Tropes. <https://tvtropes.org/pmwiki/pmwiki.php/Main/SubvertedTrope>
- "Double Subversion." TV Tropes. <https://tvtropes.org/pmwiki/pmwiki.php/Main/DoubleSubversion>
- "Lampshade Hanging." TV Tropes. <https://tvtropes.org/pmwiki/pmwiki.php/Main/LampshadeHanging>
- Laws, Robin D. *Robin's Laws of Good Game Mastering*. Steve Jackson Games, 2002. (Player types and NPC role design for interactive play.)
- Costikyan, Greg et al. *Paranoia* (tabletop role-playing game). West End Games, 1984; Mongoose Publishing editions. (Comedy-dystopia archetype play and the open-traitor roster.)

<!-- BACKLINKS:START (generated by scripts/backlinks.py — do not edit by hand) -->

## Referenced by

- [Playbook Design](../systems-mechanics/playbook-design.qmd)

<!-- BACKLINKS:END -->
